I agree with this updated explanation but want to add another point. The prevalence of Rh factor sensitivity means that many women carry some level of anti-D antibodies naturally due to various exposures throughout life, even before pregnancy. Therefore, for non-sensitized mothers, the risk posed by anti-D antibodies can be lower compared to other factors like anti-C or anti-E where fewer individuals may already harbor those particular antibodies. Nonetheless, in cases specifically mentioning lack of sensitization, option A remains appropriately selected because the primary concern hinges on the potential severity introduced by pre-existing immunity rather than natural levels present regardless of sensitization status.
